task: I’ve got a basement plumbing issue that needs a repair crew to come inside, and I want to make sure the job gets done without the work dragging all over the house. What should I have ready before they arrive, and what should I keep an eye on while they’re working?

turns.agent_response:
Have the space and the info ready, and keep the job contained.

## Before they arrive
- Clear a path to the work area
- Move anything fragile or valuable out of the way
- Know where the **main shutoff** is
- Know where any **local shutoffs** are
- Take photos of the area first
- Note any existing damage, stains, leaks, or soft spots
- Keep pets/kids out of the work zone

## Have ready
- the estimate / scope
- access instructions
- your contact number
- any past repair info
- photos of the problem
- towels/bucket if there’s active leaking

## While they’re working
Watch for:
- whether they stay in the agreed area
- whether they shut off water before opening lines
- whether they protect floors and walls
- whether they keep materials and debris contained
- whether they start talking about “while we’re here” extras

## Stop them if
- they want to open up more area without explaining why
- they start adding work that wasn’t discussed
- they can’t explain what changed
- they’re about to cut into something you didn’t approve

## Good questions to ask
- What exactly are you fixing?
- What area will you need access to?
- Is this contained to the basement?
- Will any walls, ceilings, or floors be opened?
- Is anything extra needed before you continue?

## End-of-job check
Before they leave, confirm:
- the leak/problem is actually fixed
- the area is cleaned up
- any openings are covered or patched as agreed
- you know what was replaced
- you have the receipt and warranty info

If you want, I can turn this into a **basement repair prep checklist** you can print.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the basics. But I’d want it tighter than that.

Before they show up:
- main shutoff located and tested
- basement path cleared, nothing valuable down there
- old towels, bucket, flashlight handy
- estimate in writing, not just some mouthy promise
- any prior leak spots marked so they don’t play dumb
- receipts and phone right there

While they’re working:
- don’t let them wander
- keep an eye on whether they actually protect the floor and wall
- make them show you what’s open before they cut more
- if they say “while we’re here,” that’s where the bill gets stupid
- ask what changed if the scope grows

And I’d add one thing: don’t leave them alone with the whole house if you can help it. I had a freeze break a line a few winters back, and the crew wanted me out of the way. I stayed right there and checked every step. Saved me from a mess and a bigger bill.

If you want, I can make it into a short no-nonsense checklist you can keep by the phone.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — here’s the tight version.

## Basement plumbing repair: prep and watch list

### Before they arrive
- **Main shutoff located and tested**
- Any **local shutoffs** located too
- **Basement path cleared**
- **Valuables moved out**
- **Towels, bucket, flashlight** ready
- **Estimate/scope in writing**
- **Old leak spots marked or photographed**
- **Phone and receipts** kept together

### While they’re working
- Stay on the **basement job only**
- Watch that they **protect floor and wall**
- Make them **show you what’s open before more gets cut**
- If they say **“while we’re here”**, stop and ask what changed
- Don’t let the scope grow without a clear explanation

### Stop them if
- they want to open more area without a reason
- they’re not containing the mess
- they can’t explain the change in work
- they start freelancing outside the estimate

### End check
- problem is actually fixed
- area is cleaned up
- any openings are covered as agreed
- you know what was replaced
- you have the receipt and warranty info

If you want, I can make this into a **6-line pocket version**.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah. That’s tight enough.

Main shutoff, estimate, photos, tools, receipts. Keep them in the basement, not wandering around like they own the place. And if the scope starts growing, I’d stop it right there and make them explain what changed.

That’ll do.
